ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Cantonese roasted pork (siu yuk); mapped to broader pork food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Cantonese-style roasted pork; A00LC (pig meat, cooked) is the closest defensible broader match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for siu yuk / Cantonese roasted pork as of 2026.

serving.description: 1 portion (approx. 150g), suitable for 1 person as part of a shared meal for 3–4 persons
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving of roasted pork in a Singaporean hawker or restaurant context is approximately 150g, often served sliced over rice or as part of a shared platter for 3–4 people.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Pork (Siu Yuk) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Pork (Siu Yuk), cr_id G00010709. All per_100g core and secondary values are taken from the canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated_fat_g is adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168288 (pork belly, cooked) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted pork (siu yuk) contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g per HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010709). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates — pure meat products are excluded from GI classification per the University of Sydney GI Database methodology and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. GI set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable in accordance with Project Veritas zero-carbohydrate GI rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050mg per 100g — far exceeds the 600mg/100g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid roasted pork due to its very high sodium content of 1050mg/100g; a 150g serving delivers approximately 1575mg sodium, representing over 68% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate eliminates direct glycaemic impact, but saturated fat content (~3.8g/100g) and high sodium may worsen insulin resistance and cardiovascular comorbidities
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted pork has no direct glycaemic impact due to zero carbohydrate content, making it acceptable in moderate portions for blood glucose management; however, its saturated fat and very high sodium content warrant limiting frequency to avoid worsening insulin resistance and hypertension-related comorbidities common in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050mg/100g), saturated fat (~3.8g/100g), and cholesterol (89mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of roasted pork due to its combination of high sodium (1050mg/100g), saturated fat, and dietary cholesterol (89mg/100g), all of which contribute to elevated blood pressure, LDL cholesterol, and atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; high sodium worsens joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit roasted pork due to its pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content and high sodium (1050mg/100g), which can exacerbate joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Pork (Siu Yuk / 烧肉) is a classic Cantonese-style dish widely available in Singapore's hawker centres, Chinese roast meat stalls, and restaurants. It is prepared by marinating pork belly with salt, five-spice powder, white pepper, rice wine, and soy sauce, then roasting at high heat to achieve a distinctively crispy, crackling skin over tender, juicy meat layers. The dish is characterised by its savoury, slightly smoky flavour, rich umami depth from the marinade, and the textural contrast between the brittle skin and the succulent pork belly beneath. Nutritionally, roasted pork is a high-protein (19.1g/100g), moderate-fat (10.5g/100g) food with zero carbohydrate, making it glycaemically neutral. However, its sodium content is very high at 1050mg/100g, making it a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is 89mg/100g. It is commonly served sliced over steamed rice, with mustard or sweet plum sauce on the side, or as part of a mixed roast meat platter. In Singapore, it is a staple of Chinese roast meat (烧腊) cuisine and is frequently consumed at lunch and dinner. The 'Big' designation (大) in the HPB record refers to a larger portion size suitable for 3–4 persons. It is not suitable for halal or kosher diets.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in roasted pork?
Is siu yuk high in fat?
What is the calorie count of Chinese roast pork?
Is roasted pork suitable for hypertension?
What is 烧肉 in English?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010709) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as this is a pure meat product prepared without sugar-based glazes or marinades; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010709). Sugar_g is naturally zero per the same source. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a pure meat product with no plant-based components. Saturated_fat_g of 3.8g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for roasted pork belly (FDC ID 168288) as the HPB record does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this is documented as an adapted value. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not disaggregated in the HPB Singapore Foodo record for this specific entry (G00010709) as of 2026-04-17; USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168288 was consulted as a secondary reference but does not provide a complete matching profile for this Singapore-specific preparation. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g confirmed per HPB); GI set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Project Veritas zero-carbohydrate GI rule, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology for pure protein/fat foods. The 'Big' (大) designation refers to a portion size for 3–4 persons as noted in the HPB description field.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, pure meat product), sodium_mg 1050.0, saturated_fat_g 3.8 (adapted from USDA FDC ID 168288, documented in notes and nutrient_source), sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero per HPB). All four required secondary fields present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) as food contains negligible carbohydrates. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source now cites Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) and University of Sydney GI Database methodology as the approved GI authority basis for excluding pure meat products from GI classification. source_match_type is 'analogous_estimate'. Compliant with zero-carbohydrate GI rule and g2 approved-source requirement.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected gi_source.source_match_type from 'not_applicable' to 'analogous_estimate'. Corrected ontology match types. Updated zero-macro justifications in notes. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with reason and source consulted.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Fixed g2 failure — replaced non-compliant gi_source with peer-reviewed journal citation (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) and University of Sydney GI Database methodology reference, satisfying the approved GI source requirement for zero-carbohydrate foods. Fixed g3 failure — expanded notes to explicitly document all -1 sentinel fields with the specific reason (HPB record does not disaggregate these values for G00010709) and the secondary source consulted (USDA FDC ID 168288), satisfying the g3 missing-data basis documentation requirement. Updated nutrient_source.source_match_notes to reference the USDA adaptation for saturated_fat_g.

Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical entry G00010709 (confidence 0.95). All mandatory Singaporean-dish multilingual names present.
